---
title: "Day 34 — Fine-tuning: the Trainer, and the loop it hides"
subtitle: "Wednesday, 26 August 2026 · Week 5 · Pretrained models · Study day · 3-4 h"
---

::: {.day-nav .kind-study}
[← Day 33](day-33.qmd) · [All 50 days](../timeline.qmd) · [Day 35 →](day-35.qmd)
:::

::: {.why}
`Trainer.train()` is fifteen lines that do about forty things, and you write the manual loop once so that when it does one of them wrong you can find it in an afternoon instead of a week.
:::

## What you should be able to do tonight

- You have a fine-tuned checkpoint and know, on the validation split, whether it beats Day 33's prompting baseline on your primary metric and by how much.
- You can list four things `Trainer` did that your manual loop needed an explicit line for - at minimum moving the batch to the device, zeroing gradients, stepping the LR schedule, and switching to `model.eval()`.
- You can read your own loss curves and say whether the run is underfit, overfit, or stopped early, naming the epoch where validation loss turned.

## Tasks <span class='task-total'>205 min</span>

::: {.task-list}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [65 min]{.task-min} Read chapter3/3 and fine-tune with `Trainer`: a biomedical BERT-family encoder, `AutoModelForSequenceClassification` with `num_labels=5`, the `max_length` you chose on Day 32, three epochs, `eval_strategy='epoch'`, and a `compute_metrics` returning your Day 33 primary metric plus per-class F1.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [65 min]{.task-min} Rewrite the identical run as a manual loop from chapter3/4 - `DataLoader` with your collator, `AdamW`, `get_scheduler` with warmup, the forward/backward/step/zero inner loop, and `model.eval()` under `torch.no_grad()` for validation - and land within noise of the `Trainer` validation score. Hold the seed fixed so a gap means something.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [40 min]{.task-min} Read chapter3/5 (Understanding Learning Curves) and plot a two-panel figure sharing one x-axis: train and validation loss on top, primary metric below, both runs drawn on both panels.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [35 min]{.task-min} Retrain on 10%, 30%, and 100% of the training split and plot validation metric against training-set size. Write one sentence under the plot answering 'would more labels help?' - that answer decides whether you spend a day labelling before Day 41.
:::
:::

## Resources

- [HF LLM Course ch3 §3 - Fine-tuning with the Trainer API](https://huggingface.co/learn/llm-course/en/chapter3/3) [course]{.res-kind}
- [HF LLM Course ch3 §4 - A full training loop](https://huggingface.co/learn/llm-course/en/chapter3/4) [course]{.res-kind}
- [HF LLM Course ch3 §5 - Understanding Learning Curves](https://huggingface.co/learn/llm-course/en/chapter3/5) [course]{.res-kind}
- [Hugging Face Transformers docs](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers) [docs]{.res-kind}
- [Weights & Biases - run logging, same log you started at Milestone 1](https://wandb.ai/site) [tool]{.res-kind}
- [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/) [tool]{.res-kind}

## Deliverable

::: {.deliverable}
`day34_finetune.ipynb`, plus a `runs/` directory holding the `Trainer` and manual-loop checkpoints with their matching validation scores, the two-panel learning-curve figure, and the three-point data-scaling curve with your one-sentence answer on whether more labels would help.
:::

## Checkpoint

::: {.checkpoint}
Your manual loop lands 0.03 below `Trainer` on the same data and the same seed. Name the first two things you check, and say what each one would look like in the loss curve if it were the cause.
:::

## Prepare for tomorrow

Ten minutes tonight so Day 35, Thursday, 27 August 2026 starts clean. This is what the evening email sends you.

::: {.task-list .prep}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Check that `runs/` holds both checkpoints and that `baselines/prompt_v1_val.jsonl` still carries its latency and token-count columns - tomorrow's report is built out of them.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Confirm `from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve` imports cleanly.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Write one number in your log tonight: the test accuracy you expect the fine-tune to reach. You are grading that prediction tomorrow, and a miss tells you more than a hit.
:::
:::
